response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As an 85-year-old woman, I've lived through some tough times in my eighty-five years, but when it comes to COVID vaccines, I feel pretty clear-headed. My dad was a doctor back in the day, you see—PhD level—and that always helped me understand things better. Although he's not here anymore, his love for science and medicine sticks with me.

Nowadays, people talk about these vaccines all over the place. It can be overwhelming if someone like me doesn't keep up with the new stuff. But I've got my head screwed on tight—good mental health keeps things in check—and I don't want to make myself sick by getting confused or scared.

I do get a few times monthly on social media, but usually not too deep into it unless something really hits close to home. Sometimes though, when folks are chatting about COVID and vaccines online? That's where I need my rock climbing hobby for balance—gives me an escape from the noise if things feel too intense.

I've learned through experience that being agreeable helps here; you don't want arguments or stress over health matters anyway. But let’s be honest, some folks just won’t listen to reason because they haven't seen what COVID can do up close like I have (living in Philadelphia). My low extraversion means these conversations aren’t for me—unless it's one-on-one with someone who knows me well—but being a native American and self-employed carpenter gives me perspective too.

So, my advice? Listen to your body first. Then check out what experts say, just like how we did things back when my dad was teaching folks about hygiene during the polio scares.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I started from personal experience—COVID hit real hard because I'm older—and connected it with my background as a native American living in Philadelphia with firsthand knowledge. Then tied it to education influence (my PhD parent) and mental health resources. Used hobbies like rock climbing for managing stress during discussions, and linked low extraversion to preferring more private conversations.
response_parsed.tone: Reflective yet practical, drawing from life experience but grounded in everyday realities.
